sentences: Of 204 Nasdaq-100 removals since 2007, 126 (75.0% of the 168 eligible) had a usable post-removal price series; the rest were acquisitions (36) or names that left no data (42). Because the missing names skew toward the worst outcomes, the figures below are an upper bound.
The median removed stock bottomed at -12.8% versus its first close out of the index, about 54 trading days later.
Across stocks, the median indexed path never fell below its starting level and stood at +21.9% by trading day 252 (balanced panel of full-year windows).
Median one-year return was +21.9% (95% CI [+10.4%, +36.0%], n=113); 67% of full-year names were positive, but only 51% beat QQQ over the same window.
Correcting for the missing names pulls that median as low as +3.5% (a bias spread of about 15.4 points).
Outcomes tracked the market: median one-year return was +47.1% for removals during strong-bull QQQ windows versus -12.6% during crash windows.
By sector (groups with n≥5), Real Estate removals fared best (median +42.4%) and Health Care worst (median +6.0%).
And then what? 21.4% of analyzed removals later re-entered the Nasdaq-100, a median 2.49 years after leaving.
caveat: Descriptive and educational, not investment advice. Survivorship bias applies throughout: delisted and unresolved names are under-represented, so realized outcomes were likely worse.
